schools.summary: Ellicott catalogues the ranks summoned (princes of the tribes, of the courses, stewards, officers, mighty men) and notes the priests and Levites absent until v.21; Keil frames the whole design of the assembly, to secure Solomon's succession and launch the temple.
Matthew Henry reads the gathering as a servant finishing his course, a type of the Son of David growing busier near his end, taking his leave of the estates as Moses and Joshua did.
Clarke and Wesley dwell on the aged king's renewed energy, improving his recovery from the weakness of 1 Kings 1:1 to do God and his country a little more service.
Gill, brief here, identifies the assembled princes as the heads of the tribes.

Ellicott reads 'brethren' as kinship not condescension and explains the house of rest and the footstool (mercy-seat); Keil holds the phrase to be condescending goodwill and denies (against Kimchi) that David was bedridden.
Gill has David rise from bed or couch, recovered of his palsy, and recalls the ancient decorum of kings standing to address a multitude (Agamemnon).
Clarke stresses the aged, bedridden king receiving strength to rise for the nobles; Wesley grounds 'brethren' in Deut. 17:20 and contrasts God's throne in heaven with the temple as his footstool.
Matthew Henry: David, once pleased they were all his servants (1 Chron. 21:3), now calls them brethren whom he loved.

debates.question: Do the words 'my brethren, my people' express royal condescension or a genuine acknowledgment of kinship?

sides.position: condescending goodwill, a king stooping in affection to his subjects
an acknowledgment that all Israel are kin and David their brother, the humility the law requires of a king (Deut. 17:20)

schools.summary: Ellicott presses the emphatic 'God said' against David's 'I—in my heart it was'; Keil reads 'a man of war, hast shed blood' as a disqualification of office, not a charge of guilt, David having truly prepared labour and materials (1 Chron. 22:2, 14).
Gill notes the prohibition came by the mouth of Nathan the prophet.
Clarke refers the veto back to its first announcement in 2 Samuel 7:6, 13.

themes: man of war and man of peace

schools.summary: Matthew Henry lays out the descending chain of divine choices overriding human precedence: Judah not the eldest tribe (Gen. 49:10), Jesse not the senior house, David the youngest son, chosen by worth not age.
Ellicott renders 'Judah he chose for prince' (nagid) and the verb of pleasure (eudokein), noting David uses of himself the phrase spoken over the Son of David at his baptism (Matt. 3:17).
Gill marks the sovereignty of the choice over the elder brethren and reads 'for ever' of David's reign, his posterity, and his antitype Christ.
Clarke reads 'for ever' as conditional on obedience for Israel, but without end as it refers to Christ.

themes: David a type of the beloved Son

schools.summary: Ellicott dwells on the unique phrase 'the throne of the kingdom of the LORD' (cf. 1 Chron. 29:23; 17:14): the monarchy is only a vicegerency, Jehovah the true King (Gideon, Judg. 8:23; 1 Sam. 8:7).
Matthew Henry: Solomon, one of the youngest sons, was chosen because likeliest to build the temple, the wisest and best inclined.
Gill notes David's many sons, from whom the choice fell on Solomon.

themes: the throne of the kingdom of the LORD

schools.summary: Matthew Henry hears the decree of Ps. 2:7 ('Thou art my Son'): Solomon the beloved Jedidiah, a figure of the beloved Son; 'he shall build my house' points to Christ, founder and foundation of the gospel temple (Isa. 9:7; Luke 1:33).
Ellicott stresses the emphatic 'he it is that shall build' (he, not thou) and renders the adoption formula, 'I have chosen him for a son, and I will become to him a father.'
Wesley: in building God's house Solomon 'was a figure of him that was to come, both the founder and the foundation of the gospel-temple.'
Ellicott lays the parallels of the conditional promise (1 Kings 9:4; 3:14) and reads 'as at this day' with the close of Solomon's prayer (1 Kings 8:61).
Wesley distinguishes the two references: the promise is absolute for the Messiah but conditional for Solomon, whose share turns on obedience.
Matthew Henry presses the 'if': perseverance wears the crown though it wins it not; God's favour is kept by the constancy that received it.

themes: absolute for Christ, conditional for Solomon

schools.summary: Ellicott hears the Mosaic adjuration (Deut. 4:26; 30:19), reads 'seek' as never neglecting the commandments (1 Chron. 15:13), and the land-motive as an echo of Deuteronomy and Lev. 25:46.
Wesley parses 'keep' what you know and 'seek' what you do not; Clarke notes the Targum's 'before the Word of the LORD' for 'the audience of our God.'
Matthew Henry casts it as a courtroom charge, God and the congregation witnesses that fair warning was given (cf. 1 Tim. 5:21; 2 Tim. 4:1).
Gill: the congregation present represented all Israel, so the charge reached the whole covenant people.

themes: God and the assembly as witnesses
obedience the tenure of the land

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs 'know' (regard), 'perfect heart' (whole, undivided, integer) and 'willing mind' (voluntary service), and insists divine grace is not restricted by the condition, being found of them that sought him not (Isa. 65:1).

Matthew Henry joins knowledge and service inseparably, calls sincerity our gospel perfection, and reads seek/forsake as strictly reciprocal — 'God never casts any off till they have first cast him off.'
Wesley makes the heart-searching the death of hypocrisy and presses the threat even against the promises to David and his seed.

debates.question: Is finding God the strict reward of seeking, forfeited only by our own forsaking, or may grace outrun the seeker?

sides.position: strictly reciprocal — seeking finds, forsaking is cast off; God never casts any off till they first cast him off
seeking in earnest always finds, yet divine grace is not restricted even by this condition, being found of them that sought him not (Isa. 65:1)

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'See now that Jehovah hath chosen thee' — weigh the high commission, then be strong and act (cf. 1 Chron. 22:13), the call now urgent.
Matthew Henry balances the caution ('take heed', beware of evil) with the courage ('be strong, and do it', resolution and divine grace), tying the building to holiness of principle.
Wesley reads 'be strong' as courage to break through all difficulties, without which no work of God is done as it ought.
Gill reads 'take heed' as a warning against forsaking God and an alerting of the mind to the plans to come.

themes: holiness and the work made one

schools.summary: Ellicott traces the plan from outside in — porch (1 Kings 6:3), the two houses, treasuries (a Persian loan-word), upper chambers, the place of the mercy-seat — noting 'pattern' is the tabnith of Exodus 25:9.

Matthew Henry insists the pattern was given by the immediate hand of God (like Moses' in the mount, Heb. 8:5), the temple a sacred type of Christ, not to be framed by human art but by divine institution.
Clarke pictures an ichnograph with elevations and specifications, received by inspiration from God as Moses received the tabernacle plan; Wesley identifies the rooms.
Gill takes the porch as the eastward entrance at the temple's front.

themes: the house of God as God's own design

schools.summary: Clarke follows the Targum ('spirit of prophecy'); Wesley argues that as the Mosaic tabernacle was suggested by God's Spirit, God would use no less care in this greater work.
Keil takes 'by the spirit' of what David's own spirit had designed; Ellicott reads it as what had 'come into his mind,' the design as it lay there, while granting the ultimate origin is divine.
Gill reads it of the Spirit of God who gave the whole form, noting the Jewish view that Samuel was first made acquainted with it.

debates.question: Does 'by the spirit' name the Spirit of God revealing a heavenly blueprint, or David's own mind conceiving the design under God?

sides.position: the Spirit of God, the spirit of prophecy, as the pattern of the tabernacle was shown to Moses
David's own spirit — the design as it lay in his mind — though its ultimate origin is divine

themes: Spirit of God or David's mind

schools.summary: Ellicott connects the courses and the cells (against the LXX/Vulg 'description of the courses'), explains 'the work of the service' (cooking sacrificial flesh, baking shewbread), and cites the Syriac paraphrase; Keil reads one continuous construction, the cells set apart for the ministry and its vessels.
Gill notes these were the courses David had lately fixed, appointing where each should serve.

Ellicott reads the concise Hebrew as a schedule of exact weights for the golden and silver vessels (cf. Ezra 8:25); Keil marks the enumeration of vessels beginning, a word to be supplied from the 'pattern.'
Gill takes the repeated 'by weight' as divine direction, David shown by the Spirit the exact quantity for each.
Clarke: the weight named was the quantity of gold to be put in each article.
Ellicott reads a schedule of weights for the golden lampstands (cf. Exod. 25:31), and notes the silver lampstands, named nowhere else, stood by the Rabbis in the priests' chambers.
Clarke marks one chandelier in the tabernacle but ten in the temple (1 Kings 7:49); Wesley reads the closing phrase of fixed versus portable lampstands.
Gill, from Jewish authorities, gives ten candlesticks of seven lamps each, five on either side of the Mosaic stand, a hundred pounds of gold apiece.

Ellicott notes the Law's single table (Exod. 25:23; 1 Kings 7:48) and the ten golden tables (2 Chron. 4:8), and conjectures that 'shewbread' may be a gloss displacing 'gold'; the silver tables the Rabbis place in the court.
Gill: one table in the tabernacle became ten in the temple, each a hundred pounds of gold.
Wesley: divers tables about the shewbread, one more eminent, commonly called in the singular the table of the shewbread.

outliers.note: Conjectures that 'shewbread' (hamma'areketh) is a gloss displacing 'gold' (hazzahab), the original reading being 'tables of gold' to balance 'tables of silver' at the verse's end.

themes: the bread of the Presence multiplied

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies the forks, bowls, and flagons of pure gold and their uses (lustral sprinkling, libations; Exod. 25:29; Num. 4:7), and renders the 'basons' as tankards or lidded pitchers (cf. Ezra 1:10), all weighed for their offices.
Gill explains the fleshhooks as the forks for taking flesh out of the boiling pots, to be of pure gold.

themes: divine exactness to the least tool

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the cherubim (not the ark) as the chariot on which God is enthroned (Ps. 18:10; 99:1; Exod. 25:20), Keil recalling Ezekiel's wheels beneath the throne.
Wesley reads the 'refined' gold of the incense-altar as typical of Christ's intercession, and the chariot as hinting God is not fixed but would remove if forsaken.
Matthew Henry takes these to be the two greater cherubim Solomon would make (1 Kings 6:23), a chariot because the angels are the chariots of God (Ps. 68:17).
Gill: the altar of incense overlaid with refined gold, a hundred pounds by weight.

themes: God enthroned, yet free to depart

schools.summary: Gill lays out the options — an impulse of the Spirit, a prophet's writing, or an actual handwriting from the LORD through angels as Moses received the two tables — and leans to a direct divine writing.
Wesley offers both a man of God who wrote it down and God writing on the table of David's mind; Clarke prefers a vision of ecstasy, the temple's architecture of divine origin (Delaney).
Ellicott, most cautious, is unsure whether the 'writing from the hand of Jehovah' came through a seer or was merely the Mosaic description (Exod. 25:9), and allows the text may be corrupt.

debates.question: How did David receive the pattern — by direct writing from God's hand like Sinai, through a prophet, or by his own inspired mind?

sides.position: a direct handwriting from the LORD himself, a second Sinai (through the ministry of angels, or in a vision)
mediated through a prophet or seer (Samuel) who committed it to writing and delivered it to David
the mode is obscure — perhaps only the old Mosaic description, perhaps the text is corrupt

outliers.note: Alone leaves the question raw, observing that the verb 'he taught' wants an object and that perhaps the text is corrupt.

themes: the pattern in writing from God's hand
direct revelation, prophet, or corrupt text

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the resumed charge, 'and do it' added for imminent action, the Joshua-language of Deut. 31:8 and Josh. 1:5, and 'my God' recalling David's whole experience of the Helper; Keil hears in 'even my God' the memory of many perils survived (cf. 1 Chron. 22:13).
Matthew Henry: the God who owned the fathers will not fail nor forsake (Josh. 1:5; Heb. 13:5) — God never forsakes until men first forsake him.
Wesley: God never leaves any unless they first leave him; Clarke gives the Targum's 'the Word of the LORD my God will be thy assistant.'

themes: the presence of God as ground of courage
he will not fail nor forsake

schools.summary: Ellicott reads the deictic 'behold' (David pointing), the priests among the 'princes of God' (1 Chron. 24:5), and 'willing skilful man' as a freewill offerer (Exod. 35:5; cf. 1 Chron. 22:15); Keil renders 'here you have them' and notes a textual roughness some would strike.
Clarke gathers the scene into a portrait of David's completeness as a father, setting Solomon's danger, duty, and interest before him with piety and prudence united.
Matthew Henry: good work goes on when all — priests, willing and skilful workmen, princes and people — are hearty in furthering it and none secretly clogs it.
Gill notes these were the courses David had lately fixed to serve in the temple when built.

themes: the whole nation behind the builder
